title: Platform-Specific Styles
description: Tailor your UI for iOS and Android using Platform.select and file extensions.

slides.title: Why Platform-Specific? 🍏🤖
slides.content:
# Different Platforms, Different Looks

iOS and Android have different design guidelines. Sometimes you need to adjust:
- Shadows (iOS prefers shadow props, Android uses elevation)
- Fonts
- Navigation appearance
- Touch feedback

React Native provides two main ways to handle platform differences: `Platform` module and platform‑specific file extensions.

slides.title: Using Platform.select
Platform-Specific File Extensions
slides.content:
## .ios.js and .android.js

You can create separate files for each platform. For example:
- `Header.ios.js`
- `Header.android.js`

Then import `Header` normally; React Native will pick the correct file.

This is useful when the entire component implementation differs significantly.

```jsx
// In App.js
import Header from './Header'; // loads the right one automatically
```

quizData.question: Which method allows you to define platform-specific values in a style object?
quizData.explanation: `Platform.select` takes an object with `ios` and `android` keys and returns the value for the current platform.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: For shadows, use `shadow*` props on iOS and `elevation` on Android. Remember that `elevation` also affects z‑order and touch feedback.
